title: Shared C++ tooling context and process lifecycle
summary: Assembly, probes, and generated-result UIs share compile-database discovery, argument sanitation, Tree-sitter context, latest-result-wins jobs, and scratch-window primitives.
details: cpp_tools/context.lua locates compile_commands.json upward, caches decoded databases by file metadata, resolves relative entries, and provides compiler fallbacks.
Assembly and link modes strip the original input, output, dependency, compile-only, and optimization flags before adding deterministic mode-specific tails.
The same Tree-sitter ancestry identifies source functions, qualified namespace names, structs/classes, and enums for all higher-level tools.
cpp_tools/jobs.lua cancels an older keyed process and ignores stale callbacks, so slow compilation cannot overwrite newer user intent.
cpp_tools/scratch.lua owns reusable nofile result buffers, split creation, immutable content replacement, and close/action mappings.
rationale: Independent compile_commands and syntax-context implementations had already diverged inside the assembly module and would have multiplied with each new tool.
A narrow shared layer leaves domain policy in assembly, runner, and code generation while centralizing high-risk lifecycle mechanics.

evidence.claim: Quoted commands, compile/link sanitation, qualified syntax context, and stale-process suppression are pinned.

questions: Where should a new C++ tool obtain compiler flags and function context?
How are stale compiler callbacks prevented?
Which module owns reusable result splits?

last_verified.method: Focused pure and asynchronous process tests.
